questions.stem: A pharmacology student is learning that pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics describe two different aspects of drug action. Which of the following statements best characterizes pharmacokinetics?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The biological and molecular actions the drug exerts on the body

choices.label: B
choices.text: The time course of what the body does to the drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: The interaction of a drug with its specific cellular receptor

choices.label: D
choices.text: The dose-response relationship of a drug at its target tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mechanism by which a drug produces its therapeutic effect

explanation.summary: Pharmacokinetics is what the body does to the drug over time, summarized as ADME.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pharmacokinetics describes the time course of a drug in the body through absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME). It is often summarized as what the body does to the drug, in contrast to pharmacodynamics.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The biological and molecular actions a drug exerts on the body define pharmacodynamics, not pharmacokinetics.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Interaction of a drug with its receptor is a pharmacodynamic concept.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The dose-response relationship at the target tissue is part of pharmacodynamics.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mechanism by which a drug produces its effect is pharmacodynamics.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman is prescribed estrogen for menopausal hot flashes. Her physician notes that when the drug is swallowed, a large fraction is inactivated in the liver before it reaches the systemic circulation. Which of the following medications would most likely undergo extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism?

explanation.summary: Orally administered drugs are absorbed from the GI tract into the hepatic portal vein and pass through the liver first, undergoing first-pass metabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: After oral absorption, the drug travels through the hepatic portal vein directly to the liver, where drug-metabolizing enzymes can inactivate up to about 90 percent of the drug before it reaches the bloodstream. This first-pass effect lowers the bioavailability of oral drugs to an average F of about 0.7.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Intramuscular drugs are absorbed from muscle into the systemic circulation and bypass the hepatic portal vein, avoiding first-pass metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Intravenous drugs enter the systemic circulation directly, so they are not subject to first-pass metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Subcutaneous drugs are absorbed into the systemic circulation and do not pass through the portal vein first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transdermal drugs are absorbed through the skin into the systemic circulation and avoid the first-pass effect.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient requires a drug with an immediate and precisely controlled serum concentration, and the pharmacist selects a route that delivers the entire dose to the systemic circulation. Which route of administration has a bioavailability (F) of 100 percent?

explanation.summary: Intravenous injection delivers the drug directly into the systemic circulation, giving a bioavailability of 100 percent.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because IV drugs are injected directly into the systemic circulation, 100 percent of the administered dose is available and F equals 1.0. IV administration also provides a rapid effect and a precise serum drug level.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral bioavailability is less than 100 percent because of incomplete absorption and first-pass metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subcutaneous injection must cross absorption barriers, so its bioavailability is less than 100 percent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intramuscular injection depends on absorption from muscle and does not reliably reach 100 percent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inhaled drug bioavailability is reduced by absorption barriers and metabolic factors.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with angina is instructed to place a tablet under his tongue for rapid relief rather than swallowing it. Compared with the oral route, the sublingual route achieves a higher bioavailability primarily because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bypasses the hepatic portal vein and avoids first-pass metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is absorbed across the large surface area of the alveolar epithelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases the fraction of drug bound to plasma albumin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is broken down more rapidly by hepatic drug-metabolizing enzymes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uses an acid-resistant film coating that delays disintegration

explanation.summary: Sublingual drug is absorbed into venous blood of the oral cavity, which bypasses the hepatic portal vein and the liver.
explanation.whyCorrect: When placed under the tongue, the drug undergoes passive diffusion into the venous blood of the oral cavity, which bypasses the hepatic portal vein and the liver. Avoiding first-pass metabolism increases the amount of active drug reaching the systemic circulation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Absorption across the alveolar epithelium describes the inhalation route, not the sublingual route.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased protein binding would reduce the free active drug and is not the reason sublingual bioavailability is higher.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Faster hepatic metabolism would lower, not raise, bioavailability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An acid-resistant coating delays absorption and is unrelated to why sublingual dosing avoids first-pass metabolism.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A drug circulates in the plasma in two forms: one bound to plasma proteins and one that is unbound. Which form is able to leave the bloodstream, distribute into tissues, and bind its receptor to produce an effect?

explanation.summary: Only the free, unbound drug can pass from the bloodstream into tissues to interact with receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: A drug in the bloodstream exists as free drug dissolved in plasma water and as protein-bound drug. Only the free, unbound fraction can pass from the bloodstream into tissues and interact with receptors to produce the drug's effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Protein-bound drug is an inactive reservoir that remains in the bloodstream and cannot act on tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Albumin-bound drug is a specific form of protein-bound drug and is likewise inactive and confined to the circulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A depot preparation is a formulation designed for slow release, not a plasma form of the drug.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metabolized drug has already been chemically processed and is not the active form reaching receptors.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with cirrhosis and hypoalbuminemia is started on a drug that is normally highly protein-bound. Compared with a healthy person, he is at increased risk of an exaggerated drug effect. Low albumin causes this risk because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increases the free, pharmacologically active drug fraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreases the amount of unbound drug reaching the tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increases binding of the drug to plasma proteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduces the ability of the drug to cross the blood-brain barrier

choices.label: E
choices.text: Accelerates renal excretion of the free drug

explanation.summary: Reduced albumin lowers protein-binding capacity, raising the free active drug fraction and the drug effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: Albumin is the key plasma protein that binds drugs. Conditions such as liver disease and malnutrition decrease albumin levels, which lowers protein-binding capacity and increases the free, active drug fraction, leading to a greater or exaggerated effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Low albumin increases, rather than decreases, the amount of unbound drug reaching tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low albumin provides fewer binding sites, so drug binding decreases rather than increases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Albumin level does not determine passage across the blood-brain barrier, which depends on lipid solubility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A higher free fraction increases exposure and effect rather than speeding excretion in this scenario.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman takes diphenhydramine for seasonal allergies and reports feeling drowsy afterward. This central nervous system effect occurs because diphenhydramine is lipophilic enough to:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Undergo complete first-pass metabolism in the liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Be excreted unchanged by the kidneys

explanation.summary: The blood-brain barrier admits lipid-based drugs, so lipophilic diphenhydramine enters the CNS and causes drowsiness.
explanation.whyCorrect: The blood-brain barrier is a tight mesh of capillaries that protects the brain, and only lipid-based medications or those using a carrier can penetrate it. Diphenhydramine is lipophilic enough to cross the barrier and act on the central nervous system, producing drowsiness.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Binding to plasma albumin would keep the drug in the circulation and would not produce a CNS effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Complete first-pass metabolism would reduce active drug rather than explain CNS penetration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Accumulation in adipose tissue is not the mechanism by which the drug causes drowsiness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal excretion is an elimination process and does not explain crossing into the brain.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman is started on a lipophilic sedative, and her physician deliberately chooses a lower dose than would be used in a younger adult. Which age-related change most directly explains why older adults often require lower doses of such drugs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Higher total body water than younger adults

explanation.summary: Older adults have more body fat, so lipophilic drugs accumulate and have a longer duration of action, requiring lower doses.
explanation.whyCorrect: Even at the same body mass index, older adults tend to have more body fat than younger adults. Lipophilic drugs accumulate in this fatty tissue, leading to a longer duration of action, which is one reason older adults often require lower doses.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Higher total body water is characteristic of neonates and children, not older adults.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Serum albumin decreases with age rather than increasing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intestinal absorptive surface area does not increase in older adults; the elderly often have impaired GI function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gastric emptying is typically decreased, not increased, in the elderly.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A patient with asthma uses an inhaler so that the drug is delivered to the lungs. Which property of the alveolar epithelium makes inhalation an effective route for drug absorption?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its very large surface area of approximately 100 square meters

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its complete lack of any epithelial or endothelial barriers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Its direct drainage into the hepatic portal vein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Its high concentration of plasma albumin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Its acidic pH that ionizes most drugs

explanation.summary: The alveolar epithelium provides a very large surface area (about 100 square meters), allowing effective absorption of inhaled drugs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The alveolar epithelium is approximately 100 square meters in adults, providing a large surface area for absorption of inhaled substances. This large surface area allows drugs to be delivered effectively and even in small doses.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Inhaled drugs must still cross barriers including the epithelial membrane, extravascular space, and vascular endothelium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The lungs drain into the pulmonary and systemic circulation, not the hepatic portal vein.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Albumin is a plasma protein and is not a feature of the alveolar epithelium.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An acidic ionizing pH is not the property that makes inhalation effective.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: Penicillin G is stable within a pH range of approximately 6 to 8. When it is taken by mouth, a significant portion is degraded before it can be absorbed. Which factor best explains this reduced absorption?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Degradation of the drug by the acidic pH of the stomach

choices.label: B
choices.text: Extensive binding of the drug to the alveolar epithelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excessive lipophilicity preventing the drug from dissolving

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid crossing of the blood-brain barrier

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediate renal secretion of the absorbed drug

explanation.summary: The pH of the environment can impair drug absorption; acid-labile drugs like penicillin G are degraded in the stomach.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most drugs are weak acids or bases, and the pH of the environment can facilitate or impair drug absorption. Penicillin G is stable only within a pH of about 6 to 8, so the acidic environment of the stomach degrades the drug and reduces its absorption.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Binding to the alveolar epithelium relates to inhaled drugs and is irrelevant to oral penicillin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The problem with penicillin G is acid degradation, not poor dissolution from excessive lipophilicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Crossing the blood-brain barrier is a distribution issue and does not explain reduced GI absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal secretion is an excretion process, not a cause of reduced absorption in the stomach.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A patient with type 1 diabetes self-administers insulin into the fatty tissue just below the dermis rather than taking it by mouth. Which characteristic best explains why insulin is given by the subcutaneous route instead of orally?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its large molecular size prevents effective absorption in the intestinal tract

choices.label: B
choices.text: It requires the 100 percent bioavailability that only oral dosing provides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subcutaneous tissue is highly vascular and gives the fastest possible absorption

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must undergo extensive first-pass metabolism to become active

choices.label: E
choices.text: It can only cross the blood-brain barrier when swallowed

explanation.summary: The subcutaneous route is used when a drug's molecular size is too large to be absorbed effectively in the intestinal tract.
explanation.whyCorrect: Subcutaneous injection delivers the drug into the cutis below the dermis and epidermis and is used when the drug's molecular size is too large to be absorbed effectively in the intestinal tract or when better bioavailability is needed than the oral route. Insulin, heparin, and monoclonal antibodies are commonly given subcutaneously.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Oral dosing does not provide 100 percent bioavailability; only intravenous administration reaches an F of 100 percent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subcutaneous tissue has few blood vessels, so absorption is slow and sustained, not the fastest possible.
whyWrongByChoice.D: First-pass metabolism would inactivate the drug rather than activate it, and the subcutaneous route avoids it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Crossing the blood-brain barrier is unrelated, and orally administered insulin would be degraded.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A patient with severe peripheral atherosclerosis develops an infection in a poorly perfused lower extremity. Despite an adequate dose, the antibiotic is slow to reach the infected tissue. Which principle of drug distribution best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced blood flow limits delivery of the drug to the tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: The antibiotic is too hydrophilic to enter any tissue

choices.label: C
choices.text: High plasma albumin prevents the drug from ever becoming free

choices.label: D
choices.text: The blood-brain barrier is blocking drug entry to the limb

choices.label: E
choices.text: First-pass metabolism has eliminated the antibiotic

explanation.summary: Blood flow drives distribution, so blocked or reduced perfusion slows delivery of a drug to the affected tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Blood flow plays a crucial role in the distribution of medications. Blocked vessels from atherosclerosis can hinder an antibiotic from reaching an infected area, so reduced perfusion limits delivery of the drug to the poorly perfused tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The problem is reduced perfusion, not that the drug is too hydrophilic to enter tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no indication of elevated albumin, and protein binding is not the cause of the delayed delivery here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The blood-brain barrier only affects entry into the central nervous system, not a peripheral limb.
whyWrongByChoice.E: First-pass metabolism affects oral bioavailability and does not explain poor tissue perfusion.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 of 3 - Drug Absorption and Distribution

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is asked to identify the body's principal site of drug biotransformation. Which of the following organs is primarily responsible for metabolizing drugs?

explanation.summary: The liver is the main organ of drug metabolism because it holds the highest concentration of drug-metabolizing enzymes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The liver contains a high concentration of drug-metabolizing enzymes, including the cytochrome P450 system, and is known as the body's 'detoxifying' organ. While other tissues have some metabolic capability, the liver performs the bulk of hepatic biotransformation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The kidneys have some metabolic capability and are the primary organ of excretion, but not the primary site of metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The lungs contribute only a small amount of drug metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The skin has minimal drug-metabolizing capacity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The spleen is not a significant drug-metabolizing organ.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A drug given orally is absorbed through the GI tract and passes through the liver, where much of it is metabolized before reaching the systemic circulation. Which of the following routes of administration avoids this first-pass effect and yields the highest bioavailability?

explanation.summary: Intravenous administration bypasses first-pass hepatic metabolism, giving the highest bioavailability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Drugs delivered intravenously enter the bloodstream directly, so they do not pass through the GI tract and liver before reaching the systemic circulation. This avoids the first-pass effect and produces the highest bioavailability.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An oral tablet is absorbed through the GI tract and undergoes first-pass hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An oral capsule is also swallowed and subject to first-pass metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An oral solution is still absorbed enterally and passes through the liver first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nasogastric administration delivers drug to the GI tract, so it too undergoes first-pass metabolism.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: Phase I biotransformation reactions modify a drug's chemical structure through oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis. Which family of enzymes, located in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum of hepatocytes, carries out these reactions?

explanation.summary: Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes carry out Phase I oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis reactions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phase I reactions are performed by cytochrome P450 enzymes located in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum of hepatocytes. The name 'P450' reflects their peak light absorption at 450 nm, and they use heme as a cofactor to transfer electrons during oxidation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: UDP-glucuronosyltransferases perform Phase II glucuronidation, not Phase I modification.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sulfotransferases catalyze Phase II sulfation conjugation reactions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: N-acetyltransferases catalyze Phase II acetylation, a conjugation reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glutathione-S-transferases mediate Phase II conjugation, not Phase I reactions.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: Phase II biotransformation conjugates a drug with a functional chemical group to produce pharmacologically inactive metabolites that are easier to excrete. Which of the following is the most common Phase II conjugation pathway?

explanation.summary: Glucuronidation is the most common Phase II conjugation pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phase II conjugation adds an adjunct such as glucuronic acid, and glucuronidation is the most common Phase II pathway. It yields inactive, more polar metabolites that are excreted in the urine or feces.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oxidation is a Phase I modification reaction, not a Phase II conjugation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hydrolysis is a Phase I reaction, not a conjugation pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reduction is a Phase I modification reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glomerular filtration is a renal excretion mechanism, not a metabolic conjugation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man taking a medication that is a substrate of CYP3A4 is started on ketoconazole, a potent CYP inhibitor, for a fungal infection. Which of the following changes in the co-administered CYP3A4 substrate is most likely?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased serum drug level with a risk of toxicity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased serum drug level with loss of efficacy

choices.label: D
choices.text: No change in the serum drug level

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased renal excretion of the parent drug

explanation.summary: A CYP inhibitor slows metabolism of its substrate, raising serum levels and toxicity risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: CYP inhibitors reduce enzyme activity and therefore decrease the ability to metabolize substrate drugs. The substrate has a decreased rate of metabolism and increased serum drug levels, which can lead to toxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased serum levels occur with CYP inducers, not inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhibitors decrease, not increase, the rate of hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibition meaningfully changes the serum level by slowing metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The interaction alters hepatic metabolism, not renal excretion of the parent drug.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A patient stabilized on a medication that is metabolized by CYP450 enzymes is started on rifampin for tuberculosis. Rifampin is a CYP inducer. Which of the following effects on the co-administered drug is most likely?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased serum drug level with toxicity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased hepatic metabolism of the drug

explanation.summary: A CYP inducer speeds metabolism of its substrate, lowering serum levels and efficacy.
explanation.whyCorrect: CYP inducers increase enzyme synthesis or activity, so substrate drugs have an increased rate of metabolism and decreased serum drug levels. Lower levels can reduce the drug's therapeutic effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased serum levels and toxicity occur with CYP inhibitors, not inducers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inducers increase, not decrease, hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Faster metabolism shortens, rather than prolongs, the half-life.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Induction clearly changes metabolism by increasing enzyme activity.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with hypertension and high cholesterol is started on verapamil and simvastatin. His physician advises him not to drink grapefruit juice while taking these medications. Grapefruit juice interacts with these drugs by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inducing CYP enzymes, which lowers drug levels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking renal tubular secretion of the drugs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing first-pass metabolism of the drugs

explanation.summary: Grapefruit juice is a CYP inhibitor that increases the effects of simvastatin and verapamil.
explanation.whyCorrect: Grapefruit juice inhibits the CYP enzymes that metabolize simvastatin (a statin) and verapamil (a calcium channel blocker). By slowing their metabolism, it increases their serum levels and effects, raising the risk of drug-related side effects.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Grapefruit juice inhibits, rather than induces, CYP enzymes, so it raises drug levels.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The interaction involves hepatic and intestinal CYP inhibition, not renal tubular secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Grapefruit juice does not act by enhancing Phase II glucuronidation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: By inhibiting CYP enzymes it decreases, rather than increases, first-pass metabolism.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: Codeine itself has little analgesic activity; it must be converted by a CYP enzyme into morphine to relieve pain. A drug that is administered in an inactive form and requires metabolism to become active is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: A prodrug is a biologically inactive compound that must be metabolized to become active.
explanation.whyCorrect: A prodrug is administered in an inactive form and undergoes metabolism to be activated. Codeine is modified by a CYP enzyme into its active form, morphine, making it a classic prodrug example.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A CYP inducer increases enzyme activity; it is not a drug that must be activated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A CYP inhibitor reduces enzyme activity rather than describing an inactive drug awaiting activation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A conjugated metabolite is a Phase II product, typically inactive and ready for excretion, not an administered inactive drug.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A uricosuric agent increases uric acid excretion and is unrelated to the concept of drug activation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A patient with a serious infection is given probenecid together with penicillin so that the antibiotic remains at higher blood levels for a longer time. Probenecid produces this effect through which of the following mechanisms?

explanation.summary: Probenecid inhibits renal OAT transporters, decreasing tubular secretion and excretion of penicillin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Probenecid decreases renal excretion of drugs such as penicillins, cephalosporins, and methotrexate by binding to and inhibiting the organic anion transporters (OATs) in the nephron. This blocks their secretion into the renal tubules, keeping antibiotic levels higher for longer.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Probenecid affects renal tubular transport, not hepatic CYP induction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It reduces excretion by inhibiting secretion; it does not increase glomerular filtration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The mechanism involves renal tubular transporters, not biliary excretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Probenecid acts on renal transporters and does not block intestinal absorption of penicillin.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: Probenecid is also used to treat gout. It lowers blood uric acid by inhibiting tubular urate transport (for example, URAT1) so that more uric acid is excreted in the urine. This effect on uric acid handling is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uricosuric — it increases urinary excretion of uric acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: It decreases urinary excretion of uric acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: It increases tubular reabsorption of urate

choices.label: E
choices.text: It enhances hepatic metabolism of uric acid

explanation.summary: Probenecid is uricosuric: it inhibits tubular urate reabsorption, raising urinary uric acid excretion and lowering blood urate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Probenecid is a uricosuric agent that inhibits tubular urate transport, increasing the concentration of uric acid in the urine. This lowers the blood uric acid level and reduces gout symptoms such as joint swelling and pain.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Probenecid increases, not decreases, urinary uric acid excretion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It acts on renal transport, not on the production of uric acid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: It inhibits urate reabsorption; it does not increase tubular reabsorption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Its effect is on renal handling of urate, not hepatic metabolism of uric acid.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: During drug elimination, water-insoluble (lipophilic) metabolites are handled differently from water-soluble ones. Lipophilic drug metabolites are primarily excreted by which organ and route?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The liver, into the feces via bile

explanation.summary: The liver excretes lipophilic metabolites in the feces, whereas the kidney excretes hydrophilic drugs in the urine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The liver excretes water-insoluble (lipophilic) drug metabolites into the feces, secreting them via bile into the gut. In contrast, the kidneys excrete water-soluble (hydrophilic) metabolites in the urine.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The kidney excretes water-soluble (hydrophilic) drugs, not lipophilic metabolites.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The lungs account for only a small fraction of drug excretion, not the main route for lipophilic metabolites.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The skin is only a minor excretory route.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The salivary glands contribute only a small percentage of drug excretion.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with hepatic cirrhosis is prescribed a drug that undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism. Compared with a patient who has normal liver function, which change is most likely, and how should dosing be adjusted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged drug half-life; reduce the dose to avoid toxicity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased renal clearance; shorten the dosing interval

explanation.summary: Cirrhosis compromises hepatic metabolism, prolonging drug half-life and requiring a lower dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatic diseases such as cirrhosis compromise Phase I metabolic pathways, so half-lives of drugs increase and the risk of toxicity rises. A decline in plasma proteins also raises the unbound drug fraction, so doses should be reduced to maintain the same therapeutic effect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cirrhosis lengthens rather than shortens the half-life, so increasing the dose would worsen toxicity risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Impaired hepatic metabolism does change drug handling, so an adjustment is needed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cirrhosis decreases, not increases, hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The problem is impaired hepatic metabolism, not increased renal clearance.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics 1 (2 of 3): Drug Metabolism and Excretion

questions.stem: A pharmacologist is studying how an orally administered drug diffuses across the intestinal epithelial cell membrane. Which of the following forms of the drug is most able to cross the lipid bilayer of the membrane?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The form carrying the greater electric charge

explanation.summary: The unionized, uncharged form of a drug is lipophilic and can pass through the lipid bilayer.
explanation.whyCorrect: The unionized form of a drug is electrically neutral and lipophilic (lipid-loving), so it dissolves in and passes through the lipid bilayer with ease. The ionized form is charged and hydrophilic and is repelled by the lipid interior of the membrane.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The ionized form carries an electric charge and is hydrophilic, so it cannot easily cross the lipid membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The two forms are not equivalent; only the unionized form crosses easily.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A greater electric charge makes a drug more hydrophilic and less able to cross, not more.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The hydrophilic (water-loving) form is the ionized form, which is trapped by the lipid bilayer.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A weakly acidic drug (a proton donor) is dissolved in a body compartment where the surrounding pH is well below the drug's pKa. In this environment, the drug is most likely to exist in which form and undergo which fate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ionized form; readily absorbed across the membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: Unionized form; readily absorbed across the membrane

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ionized form; unable to cross the membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Unionized form; unable to cross the membrane

choices.label: E
choices.text: Equal ionized and unionized fractions; no net movement

explanation.summary: A weak acid in an acidic environment (pH < pKa) stays protonated and unionized, so it is absorbed.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an acidic environment there is a high concentration of H+, so a weak acid does not give up its proton and remains as HA, the unionized form. This neutral, lipophilic species crosses the lipid bilayer and is well absorbed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A weak acid does not ionize when pH is below its pKa, so it is not in the ionized form here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The drug is unionized in this setting, and the unionized form does cross the membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although the form (unionized) is correct, the unionized form crosses the membrane rather than being blocked.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Equal fractions occur only when pH equals pKa, not when pH is well below pKa.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: While teaching about drug ionization, a professor asks students to define pKa. The pKa of a drug is best described as the pH at which:

choices.label: C
choices.text: 50% of the drug is ionized and 50% is unionized

choices.label: D
choices.text: the drug is maximally absorbed regardless of the tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: the drug carries its greatest electric charge

explanation.summary: pKa is the pH at which a drug is 50% ionized and 50% unionized.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pKa is defined as the pH at which exactly half of the drug is ionized and half is unionized. It is one of the two factors (along with the surrounding pH) that determine a drug's ionization state and therefore its absorption.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Complete ionization occurs at pH values far from the pKa, not at the pKa itself.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Complete lack of ionization also occurs away from the pKa, not at it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: pKa is a chemical property of the drug and does not by itself define maximal absorption in every tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Maximal charge occurs when the drug is fully ionized, which is not the definition of pKa.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A weakly basic drug is present in a segment of small intestine where the luminal environment is alkaline (pH greater than the drug's pKa). Which of the following best describes the state of the drug in this environment?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fully protonated and trapped in water

explanation.summary: A weak base in an alkaline environment (pH > pKa) becomes unionized and is absorbed.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an alkaline environment with few free protons, a weak base gives up its proton (BH+ becomes B) and exists mostly in its unionized, neutral form. The unionized form is lipophilic, crosses the lipid bilayer, and is well absorbed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A weak base is ionized in acidic settings, not in an alkaline environment above its pKa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ionized form is charged and hydrophilic, so it is not readily absorbed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The unionized form is correct, but it crosses membranes well rather than being poorly absorbed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Full protonation (BH+) occurs in an acidic environment, not an alkaline one.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A weakly acidic drug is moved from an acidic environment into an alkaline environment where the pH is greater than its pKa. Compared with the acidic environment, the drug in this alkaline setting will most likely:

choices.label: A
choices.text: become unionized and cross membranes more easily

choices.label: B
choices.text: become ionized and remain in the vascular and interstitial compartments

choices.label: C
choices.text: become nonpolar and enter cells more readily

choices.label: D
choices.text: precipitate out of solution as a neutral salt

choices.label: E
choices.text: be reabsorbed more completely by passive diffusion

explanation.summary: A weak acid in an alkaline environment (pH > pKa) ionizes and stays in the aqueous compartments.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a weak acid is placed in an alkaline environment it gives up its proton and becomes the ionized form (A-). The ionized form is charged and hydrophilic, so it is not absorbed and instead remains in the vascular and interstitial fluid compartments.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A weak acid becomes ionized, not unionized, when pH rises above its pKa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ionized form is polar and hydrophilic, so it does not enter cells more readily.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ionization keeps the drug dissolved in water rather than precipitating it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The ionized, hydrophilic form is poorly reabsorbed by passive diffusion, not more completely.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: An orally administered weakly acidic drug is best absorbed in a region of the small intestine whose relatively acidic environment favors its unionized form. This region is most likely the:

explanation.summary: Weakly acidic drugs are best absorbed in the acidic environment of the proximal duodenum.
explanation.whyCorrect: The proximal duodenum, near the antral end of the stomach, has a relatively acidic environment that keeps weak acids in their unionized, absorbable form. Weakly acidic drugs are therefore best absorbed there.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The distal ileum is alkaline and favors absorption of weak bases, not weak acids.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Despite its acidity, the stomach has a thick mucus layer and limited surface area and is not the primary absorption site.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The distal colon is not a principal site of drug absorption and is not the acidic region described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The rectal mucosa is not the acidic small-intestinal region that favors weak acid absorption.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A weakly basic drug remains predominantly unionized in an alkaline luminal environment. Along the small intestine, this drug is therefore best absorbed in the:

explanation.summary: Weakly basic drugs are best absorbed in the alkaline environment of the distal ileum.
explanation.whyCorrect: The distal ileum has an alkaline environment that keeps weak bases in their unionized, lipophilic form. Because the unionized form crosses the lipid bilayer, weakly basic drugs are best absorbed there.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The proximal duodenum is relatively acidic and favors weak acids, not weak bases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The gastric antrum is strongly acidic, which would ionize and trap a weak base.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The lower esophagus is not a significant absorptive alkaline segment of the gut.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The pyloric sphincter region is near the acidic stomach outlet, not an alkaline absorptive site.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: Although the stomach provides an acidic environment that keeps many weak acids unionized, it is not the principal site of drug absorption. The main reason the small intestine, rather than the stomach, is the primary site of absorption is that the small intestine has:

choices.label: E
choices.text: slower transit that traps ionized drug

explanation.summary: The small intestine's large surface area from villi and microvilli makes it the primary absorption site.
explanation.whyCorrect: The stomach primarily functions as a storage organ, and its thick mucus layer and limited surface area make it poor for absorption. The small intestine, with its extensive villi and microvilli, has a very large surface area and is therefore the primary site of drug absorption.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A lower luminal pH describes the stomach, not the small intestine, and is not the reason absorption is greater there.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A thick mucus layer is a feature of the stomach that hinders absorption, not a feature favoring the small intestine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Parietal cells secrete acid in the stomach; they are not the reason the small intestine absorbs more.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Slower transit trapping ionized drug does not explain the small intestine's superior absorption; surface area does.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with osteosarcoma is receiving high-dose methotrexate, a weakly acidic drug. To protect him from toxicity, the team also gives intravenous sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize his urine. Alkalinizing the urine enhances methotrexate elimination primarily because it:

choices.label: A
choices.text: keeps methotrexate unionized so it is reabsorbed by the renal tubules

choices.label: B
choices.text: converts methotrexate to its ionized form, trapping it in the urine for excretion

choices.label: C
choices.text: neutralizes methotrexate, inactivating it within the bloodstream

choices.label: D
choices.text: increases the lipid solubility of methotrexate, speeding hepatic metabolism

choices.label: E
choices.text: lowers the urine pH below the drug's pKa

explanation.summary: Alkalinizing the urine ionizes the weak acid methotrexate so it stays in water and is excreted.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sodium bicarbonate is a base that raises urine pH above methotrexate's pKa. Because methotrexate is a weak acid, it becomes ionized (charged) in the alkaline urine; the charged form stays dissolved in water, is not reabsorbed, and is rapidly excreted by the kidneys, reducing toxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Keeping methotrexate unionized would promote reabsorption and retention, the opposite of the intended effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bicarbonate alters the drug's ionization and excretion; it does not chemically inactivate methotrexate in the blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ionization makes the drug more hydrophilic (water-soluble), not more lipid-soluble, and the mechanism is renal excretion, not hepatic metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sodium bicarbonate raises urine pH; lowering it would keep the weak acid unionized and reabsorbed.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A drug has a pKa of 6.5. When this drug is placed in a solution whose pH is exactly 6.5, the relationship between its ionized and unionized forms is best described as:

choices.label: D
choices.text: entirely determined by the drug's molecular weight

choices.label: E
choices.text: shifted completely toward the charged species

explanation.summary: When the surrounding pH equals the pKa, the drug is 50% ionized and 50% unionized.
explanation.whyCorrect: By definition, the pKa is the pH at which half the drug is ionized and half is unionized. At a pH of 6.5 for a drug with a pKa of 6.5, the concentrations of the ionized and unionized forms are equal (for a weak acid, [HA] = [A-]).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Near-complete ionization occurs when pH is well above the pKa, not equal to it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Near-complete lack of ionization occurs when pH is well below the pKa, not equal to it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ionization at a given pH depends on the pKa relationship, not on molecular weight.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A complete shift toward the charged form requires a pH far from the pKa, not pH equal to pKa.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A weakly basic drug enters the strongly acidic environment of the stomach, where the pH is far below the drug's pKa. In this setting the drug will most likely:

choices.label: A
choices.text: give up a proton and become unionized

choices.label: B
choices.text: gain a proton, become ionized, and be poorly absorbed

choices.label: C
choices.text: become lipophilic and rapidly cross the gastric mucosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: remain neutral and diffuse freely into cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: be excreted unchanged in gastric secretions

explanation.summary: A weak base in an acidic environment gains a proton, becomes ionized, and is poorly absorbed.
explanation.whyCorrect: In an acidic environment there is a high concentration of H+, so a weak base accepts a proton and becomes the ionized form (BH+). This charged, hydrophilic species cannot cross the lipid bilayer easily and is therefore poorly absorbed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A weak base gains rather than gives up a proton in an acidic environment, so it becomes ionized, not unionized.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ionized form is hydrophilic, not lipophilic, so it does not rapidly cross the mucosa.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The drug becomes charged (ionized) here, so it does not remain neutral or diffuse freely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Poor absorption in the stomach does not mean the drug is excreted unchanged in gastric secretions.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: Which of the following statements correctly describes the ionized form of a drug relative to its unionized form?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is charged and hydrophilic and cannot easily cross the lipid bilayer

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is nonpolar and crosses membranes readily

choices.label: D
choices.text: It predominates when a weak acid is in an acidic environment

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is the form responsible for passive diffusion through membranes

explanation.summary: The ionized form is charged, hydrophilic, and cannot easily cross the lipid bilayer.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ionized form of a drug carries an electric charge, which makes it hydrophilic (water-loving). Because the lipid bilayer excludes charged, polar molecules, the ionized form cannot cross the membrane easily, unlike the neutral unionized form.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Uncharged and lipophilic describes the unionized form, not the ionized form.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nonpolar and membrane-permeable describes the unionized form; the ionized form is polar.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A weak acid in an acidic environment is predominantly unionized, not ionized.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Passive diffusion across the lipid bilayer is carried out by the unionized form, not the ionized form.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: pH and pKa

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with asthma develops acute wheezing and shortness of breath. She uses a rescue inhaler containing albuterol, and her symptoms improve within minutes. Albuterol relieves her symptoms because at beta-2 receptors in the lungs it acts as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A competitive antagonist that blocks the receptor

choices.label: B
choices.text: An agonist that activates the receptor to cause bronchodilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A ligand that binds but produces no cellular effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: An enzyme that degrades the receptor

choices.label: E
choices.text: A second messenger released downstream of the receptor

explanation.summary: An agonist binds a receptor and activates it; albuterol is a beta-2 agonist that causes bronchodilation.
explanation.whyCorrect: An agonist binds a receptor and 'activates' or induces its associated cellular action. Albuterol is a beta-2 receptor agonist used in asthma because it activates beta-2 receptors in the lungs to cause bronchodilation, opening the airways.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An antagonist blocks receptor function; albuterol instead activates the receptor and would not relieve bronchoconstriction if it blocked it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A ligand that binds without producing an effect would not open the airways; albuterol produces a clear cellular action.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Albuterol binds and activates the receptor as a ligand; it is not an enzyme that degrades the receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Second messengers act downstream inside the cell; albuterol is the receptor-binding drug, not the messenger.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: In pharmacodynamics, a molecule that binds to a receptor and blocks its function, competing for the binding site and preventing the receptor's normal cellular action, is best described as which of the following?

explanation.summary: An antagonist blocks receptor function by binding or competing for the binding site.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines an antagonist as a molecule that blocks a receptor's function and its associated cellular action by binding or competing for binding. This describes the blocking role exactly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An agonist binds and activates (induces) the cellular action rather than blocking it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ligands may be endogenous or exogenous and can either activate or block a receptor; the specific blocking role is antagonism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A second messenger acts downstream of the receptor and is not the blocking molecule.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A nuclear receptor is a type of receptor, not a molecule that blocks one.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: Two drugs, X and Y, produce the same maximal effect at the same receptor. On a semi-log dose-response plot, drug X reaches its EC50 at 1 nM whereas drug Y reaches its EC50 at 100 nM. Which of the following statements is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drug X is more potent than drug Y

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug Y is more potent than drug X

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drug X has greater efficacy than drug Y

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug Y has greater efficacy than drug X

choices.label: E
choices.text: The two drugs have identical potency

explanation.summary: The drug with the lower EC50 is more potent because less drug is needed to produce the effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: Potency is determined from EC50: a lower EC50 means less drug is required to achieve 50% of the maximal effect. Drug X (EC50 = 1 nM) reaches its half-maximal effect at a lower concentration than drug Y (100 nM), so drug X is more potent.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug Y has the higher EC50, so it needs more drug and is less potent, not more potent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Efficacy reflects the maximal effect (Emax), which is stated to be equal for both drugs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both drugs share the same maximal effect, so neither has greater efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Different EC50 values (1 nM vs 100 nM) mean the drugs differ in potency.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: On a graded dose-response curve, drug A plateaus at a higher maximal effect than drug B even at saturating concentrations. This indicates that drug A has greater which of the following?

explanation.summary: The height of the maximal-effect plateau (Emax) reflects efficacy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Efficacy is a measure of the maximal response produced by a drug, read as the Emax plateau of the curve. A drug whose curve plateaus at a higher Emax has greater efficacy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Potency relates to the EC50 (amount of drug needed), not to the height of the plateau.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Affinity is how tightly the drug binds its receptor and is not read as the plateau height.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Selectivity refers to producing one main effect at an appropriately high concentration, not maximal effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Specificity means having a single effect across all biological systems, unrelated to Emax.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: A student plots the same dose-response data two ways: once with drug concentration on a linear scale and once on a semi-logarithmic scale. Which of the following correctly describes the shapes obtained?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The linear-scale plot is hyperbolic and the semi-log plot is sigmoidal

choices.label: C
choices.text: The linear-scale plot is sigmoidal and the semi-log plot is hyperbolic

explanation.summary: Plotted linearly a dose-response curve is hyperbolic; plotted on a semi-log scale it becomes sigmoidal (S-shaped).
explanation.whyCorrect: On a linear scale the dose-response relationship is hyperbolic, reaching a maximum at high concentrations because of receptor saturation. On a semi-log scale (log concentration vs effect) it becomes sigmoidal, which is preferred because it makes the EC50 easier to determine accurately.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neither plot is a straight line; both are curved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The shapes are reversed: linear gives the hyperbola and semi-log gives the sigmoid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The linear-scale plot is hyperbolic, not sigmoidal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The semi-log plot is sigmoidal, not hyperbolic.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: The potency of a drug is commonly quantified using its EC50. The EC50 is best defined as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The maximal effect the drug can produce

choices.label: B
choices.text: The concentration that saturates all available receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: The concentration of drug that produces 50% of the maximal effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fraction of receptors bound at equilibrium

choices.label: E
choices.text: The dose that produces a toxic effect in 50% of subjects

explanation.summary: EC50 is the concentration producing 50% of the maximal (half-maximal) effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines EC50 as the concentration that produces 50% of Emax, or the drug's half-maximal effect. It is used to determine potency and is best read on the linear portion of the curve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The maximal effect is Emax (efficacy), not EC50.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Saturation of all receptors occurs at high concentrations that give the plateau, not at the half-maximal point.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The fraction of receptors bound relates to affinity/occupancy, not to the definition of EC50.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A toxic effect in 50% of subjects describes a toxic dose, not the EC50 for effect.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: Albuterol binds the beta-2 adrenergic receptor with a dissociation constant (Kd) of 100 nM. Compared with a second drug that binds the same receptor with a Kd of 10 nM, albuterol has which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Higher affinity because its Kd is higher

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same affinity as the second drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: Greater potency because of its higher Kd

explanation.summary: A higher Kd reflects weaker binding and therefore lower affinity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Affinity is inversely proportional to Kd (affinity = 1/Kd). A higher Kd value means weaker binding and lower affinity. Albuterol's Kd of 100 nM is higher than the other drug's 10 nM, so albuterol binds the beta-2 receptor with lower affinity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A higher Kd means lower, not higher, affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The Kd values differ (100 nM vs 10 nM), so the affinities are not the same.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Kd reflects binding strength, not the maximal effect that defines efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lower affinity tends to raise EC50 and lower potency, so a higher Kd does not increase potency.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: A patient with tachycardia is treated with a beta-blocker chosen to act on beta-1 receptors while largely sparing beta-2 receptors. Which of the following drugs best fits this selective beta-1 blocking profile?

explanation.summary: Metoprolol is a selective beta-1 blocker used for an increased heart rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes metoprolol as a selective beta-1 blocker, used in cases of increased heart rate (tachycardia), whereas propranolol is non-selective. Metoprolol therefore fits a beta-1-selective profile.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Albuterol is a beta-2 agonist, not a beta-blocker.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lisinopril is a hypertension medication, not a beta-blocker.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspirin reduces clotting and is unrelated to beta-adrenergic receptors.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: A drug that produced only one effect across all biological systems would be called specific, but in reality most drugs produce one main effect only at an appropriately high concentration. This more realistic property is termed which of the following?

explanation.summary: Selectivity is producing one main effect at an appropriately high concentration; specificity (a single effect on all systems) is rarely achieved.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick distinguishes specificity (one effect on all biological systems) from selectivity. Because the vast majority of drugs are selective rather than specific, producing their effect at an appropriately high concentration, the described property is selectivity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Specificity means a single effect across all systems, which the stem says is rarely achieved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Efficacy is the maximal response a drug can produce.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Potency is the amount of drug needed to reach an established effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Affinity is the extent to which a drug binds its receptor.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: A patient takes an antacid to neutralize gastric acid. Applying the concept of a drug's effect as the downstream consequence of a drug-target interaction, the expected result is which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activation of beta-2 receptors in the lungs

explanation.summary: Neutralizing stomach acid raises stomach pH; this is the downstream drug effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: A drug's effect describes the downstream consequences of a drug-target interaction. The brick uses the example that a drug that neutralizes stomach acid should have the effect of increasing stomach pH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neutralizing acid raises pH; it does not lower it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Activating beta-2 receptors is the mechanism of a bronchodilator, a separate example unrelated to gastric acid.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inhibiting dopamine receptors is a different example used to illustrate decreased receptor activity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreasing G-protein activity is another separate example, not the effect of neutralizing stomach acid.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with hypertension and a myocardial infarction 5 years ago is scheduled for minor knee surgery. His medications are lisinopril, metoprolol, and aspirin. The surgical resident explains that one medication should be stopped about 7 days before surgery to reduce the risk of excessive bleeding. Which medication should be stopped?

choices.label: E
choices.text: None of his medications need to be stopped

explanation.summary: Aspirin reduces clotting and increases bleeding risk, so it is held roughly 7 days before surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the case, aspirin is taken to reduce clotting risk but can increase the risk of bleeding. It is stopped about 1 week (7 days) before surgery and restarted post-discharge, so aspirin is the medication to hold.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lisinopril treats his hypertension and is not the source of the bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Metoprolol controls his heart rate and does not drive the bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neither lisinopril nor metoprolol is the antiplatelet agent; only aspirin affects clotting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspirin does increase bleeding risk and needs to be held before surgery.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: Receptors that a drug may bind include G-protein-coupled receptors, tyrosine kinase (catalytic) receptors, and nuclear receptors. A molecule, whether produced by the body or introduced from outside it, that binds to such a receptor is termed which of the following?

explanation.summary: A ligand is a molecule that binds a receptor and may be endogenous or exogenous.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines a ligand as a molecule that binds to a receptor and notes that ligands can be endogenous or exogenous to the body. Agonists and antagonists are both ligands.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A substrate is bound and converted by an enzyme; a ligand is defined by binding a receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A second messenger acts downstream inside the cell, not as the receptor-binding molecule.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Emax is the maximal effect of a drug, not a binding molecule.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The dissociation constant (Kd) is a measure of binding strength, not a molecule.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 1: Receptor Agonists and Antagonists

questions.stem: During a pharmacology lecture, a student is told that a certain antibiotic has a minimum effective concentration of 2 mg/L and a minimum toxic concentration of 9 mg/L. What is the size of this drug's therapeutic window?

explanation.summary: The therapeutic window is the range of plasma concentrations between the minimum effective concentration and the minimum toxic concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: The therapeutic window spans from the minimum effective concentration (2 mg/L) up to the minimum toxic concentration (9 mg/L). Its size is therefore 9 - 2 = 7 mg/L, the range in which the drug is both effective and non-toxic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 2 mg/L is the minimum effective concentration itself, not the width of the window.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 4.5 mg/L is roughly the midpoint of the two values, not the range between them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 9 mg/L is the minimum toxic concentration, the upper boundary rather than the window size.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 11 mg/L is the sum of the two concentrations, which has no physiologic meaning here.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A pharmaceutical company is preparing to move a new compound from animal studies into first-in-human trials and wants to calculate the drug's margin of safety to help set the starting dose. Which pair of parameters is most appropriate for this calculation?

explanation.summary: The margin of safety compares the dose effective in nearly all subjects with the dose lethal to a very small fraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The margin of safety is calculated as LD1 divided by ED99: it relates the dose that is therapeutic in 99% of animals to the dose that is lethal in just 1%. This preclinical measure is used to help set safe starting doses when moving into human trials.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ED50 and TD50 give the therapeutic index, which uses median (50%) doses rather than the extremes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ED50 and LD50 are both median doses and do not capture the safety at the tails of the distributions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ED50 and LD1 mixes a median effective dose with an extreme lethal dose and is not the defined ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ED99 and TD50 pairs an extreme effective dose with a median toxic (not lethal) dose and is not used for margin of safety.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after swallowing a bottle of his mother's iron supplements. He is treated with deferoxamine, a chelator that binds free iron so it can be eliminated by the kidneys. This drug's action is best classified as which of the following?

explanation.summary: Chemical antagonism occurs when one substance directly binds and inactivates another without involving a receptor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deferoxamine directly binds (chelates) free iron to inactivate it and promote its elimination. This is chemical antagonism, in which one agent inactivates another through a direct chemical interaction rather than acting at a drug receptor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Physiologic antagonism involves two agents producing opposite effects through different physiologic pathways, not one binding the other.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Irreversible receptor antagonism requires binding to a drug receptor; iron chelation involves no receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An inverse agonist binds a receptor and produces an effect opposite to that of an agonist; no receptor is involved here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Competitive antagonism is reversible blockade at a shared receptor site, which does not describe metal chelation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman taking warfarin daily for atrial fibrillation must return to an anticoagulation clinic every week for blood testing and dose adjustment. Which property of warfarin best explains this need for frequent monitoring?

explanation.summary: Drugs with a narrow therapeutic index have effective and toxic concentrations close together and require close monitoring.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin has a narrow therapeutic index, meaning its effective and toxic concentrations are in close proximity. Too little leaves the patient prone to clotting and too much causes bleeding, so plasma levels must be monitored weekly to keep the patient within the safe dosing window.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A wide therapeutic window would reduce the need for monitoring, the opposite of warfarin's situation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A high margin of safety implies a large gap between effective and harmful doses, which would not require frequent testing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A high median lethal dose alone would make a drug safer, not mandate weekly monitoring.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Low potency relates to the dose needed for an effect, not to the closeness of toxic and effective concentrations.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with type 1 diabetes is found unconscious after accidentally injecting too much insulin. Paramedics administer glucagon, which raises his blood glucose by promoting glycogen breakdown and opposing insulin's glucose-lowering effect. The relationship between glucagon and insulin is an example of which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Competitive antagonism at a shared receptor

explanation.summary: Physiologic antagonism occurs when two agents produce opposite physiologic effects through different pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: Insulin lowers blood glucose while glucagon raises it, each acting through its own pathway rather than a shared receptor. This opposing action on the same physiologic outcome is physiologic (functional) antagonism, which helps maintain glucose homeostasis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chemical antagonism would require one hormone to directly bind and inactivate the other, which does not occur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Insulin and glucagon act at their own distinct receptors, not a single shared receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Additive agonism describes two agents acting in the same direction, whereas these hormones oppose each other.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A pharmacokinetic interaction alters absorption, distribution, metabolism, or excretion, not opposing physiologic effects.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A new analgesic has a median effective dose (ED50) of 10 mg and a median toxic dose (TD50) of 250 mg. What is this drug's therapeutic index?

explanation.summary: The therapeutic index is the ratio of the median toxic dose to the median effective dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Therapeutic index = TD50 / ED50 = 250 mg / 10 mg = 25. A larger value indicates a wider separation between toxic and effective doses and thus a relatively safer drug.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.04 is ED50 divided by TD50, the ratio inverted.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 240 is the difference between the two doses, not their ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 260 is the sum of the two doses, which is not how the index is calculated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 2500 is the product of the two doses rather than their ratio.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: Two drugs, X and Y, relieve pain through the same receptor. On their graded dose-response curves, drug X reaches the same maximal effect as drug Y but does so at a lower concentration (its EC50 is smaller). Which statement best describes drug X compared with drug Y?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drug X is more potent but has equal efficacy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug X is more efficacious but less potent

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drug X has greater affinity but lower efficacy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug X has a higher Emax than drug Y

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drug X necessarily has a larger therapeutic index

explanation.summary: Potency is reflected by EC50, while efficacy is reflected by the maximal response (Emax).
explanation.whyCorrect: Because drug X produces its effect at a lower concentration (smaller EC50), it is more potent. Because it reaches the same maximal effect as drug Y, the two drugs have equal efficacy. Potency and efficacy are independent properties.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug X reaches the same maximal effect, so it is not more efficacious; it is more, not less, potent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both drugs reach the same maximal response, so drug X does not have lower efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Emax is the maximal effect, which is equal for both drugs by the stem.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Therapeutic index depends on toxic and effective doses and cannot be inferred from potency alone.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: In comparing two drugs, a clinician notes that at very high doses drug A produces a greater maximal response than drug B. Which pharmacodynamic property distinguishes these two drugs?

explanation.summary: Efficacy is the ability of a drug to produce a maximum response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Efficacy describes the maximal effect a drug can produce, quantified by Emax. A drug that reaches a greater maximal response at high doses has greater efficacy; differences in efficacy are evaluated by comparing maximal responses at high doses.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Potency refers to the amount of drug needed for a given effect (compared by EC50), not the size of the maximal response.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Affinity is how well a drug binds its receptor, not the magnitude of the maximal effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Therapeutic index is a safety ratio, not a measure of maximal response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Margin of safety is a preclinical safety measure, unrelated to maximal effect.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: During a lecture on drug-receptor interactions, a professor defines a property as the tenacity with which a drug binds to its receptor, a step that occurs before any downstream response is produced. Which property is being described?

explanation.summary: Affinity is how well, or how tightly, a drug binds to its receptor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Affinity is defined as the tenacity with which a drug binds to its receptor. It describes the binding step itself; efficacy is what happens after the drug is bound to the receptor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Efficacy is the response produced after binding, not the binding itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Potency is the amount of drug needed for a given effect, compared using EC50 or ED50.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Selectivity refers to preference among different targets, not the strength of binding to one receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tolerance is a decreased response with repeated dosing, unrelated to binding tenacity.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A researcher wants to determine the median dose of a drug that will cause hiccups within 6 hours in a large group of volunteers, recording only whether each person does or does not develop hiccups. Which type of dose-response relationship is being studied?

explanation.summary: Quantal dose-response curves measure all-or-none outcomes across a population.
explanation.whyCorrect: The outcome (hiccups) is present or absent in each individual and cannot be graded, and it is measured across a large population. This all-or-none, population-level relationship is a quantal dose-response study, which is used to determine a median effective dose such as the ED50 for causing hiccups.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A graded dose-response curve plots a continuous effect against concentration in a single patient, not an all-or-none population outcome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A time-response relationship tracks effect over time, not the proportion of a population responding.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Log-linear potency is not a defined dose-response study of a population outcome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Concentration-clearance describes elimination pharmacokinetics, not a quantal effect.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: Two antihypertensive drugs are compared. Drug P has a therapeutic index of 3, while drug Q has a therapeutic index of 40. Which of the following is the most accurate conclusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drug Q has greater separation between its effective and toxic doses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug P is more effective because it works at a lower dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Drug Q is more potent than drug P

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug P requires less monitoring than drug Q

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drug Q has a smaller therapeutic window than drug P

explanation.summary: A larger therapeutic index means less overlap between the effective and toxic dose-response curves, indicating greater relative safety.
explanation.whyCorrect: The therapeutic index reflects the separation between the toxic and effective doses. Drug Q's much larger index (40 versus 3) means its efficacy and toxicity curves overlap less, so it is relatively safer and generally needs less intensive monitoring.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The therapeutic index says nothing about which drug is more effective or works at a lower dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Potency is judged by EC50 or ED50, not by the therapeutic index.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This is reversed: drug P, with the narrow index of 3, requires more monitoring, not less.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A larger index generally corresponds to a wider, not smaller, margin between effective and toxic levels.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A physician starts a patient on the lowest dose of a drug that produces clinical benefit; below this plasma concentration the drug is ineffective. What is the term for this lowest concentration that provides a therapeutic effect?

explanation.summary: The minimum effective concentration is the lowest plasma concentration at which a drug shows clinical benefit.
explanation.whyCorrect: The minimum effective concentration is defined as the lowest plasma concentration at which a drug produces a clinical benefit; it forms the lower boundary of the therapeutic window. Below it, the drug is ineffective.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The minimum toxic concentration is the level above which toxic effects appear, the upper boundary of the window.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The median effective dose (ED50) is a population dose measure, not a plasma concentration threshold in one patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Steady-state concentration is when input equals elimination, not specifically the threshold for benefit.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Peak plasma concentration is the highest level reached after a dose, not the minimum needed for effect.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 2: Quantal Dose Response and Therapeutic Index

questions.stem: A 70-kg man receives a single 500-mg intravenous bolus of a drug. Immediately after administration, the peak plasma concentration (Cmax) is measured at 10 mg/L. Which of the following best approximates the drug's volume of distribution?

explanation.summary: Volume of distribution equals the dose administered divided by the peak plasma concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vd = dose / Cmax = 500 mg / 10 mg/L = 50 L. A Vd this far above blood volume indicates the drug distributes widely into tissues rather than remaining in the plasma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A Vd of 5 L would require a Cmax of 100 mg/L, ten times higher than measured.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A Vd of 25 L would require a Cmax of 20 mg/L, not 10 mg/L.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A Vd of 100 L would require a Cmax of 5 mg/L; here the concentration is higher, so the calculated volume is smaller.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A Vd of 500 L would require a Cmax of only 1 mg/L, far below the measured value.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient is to receive a continuous intravenous infusion of an antibiotic that follows first-order elimination. The desired steady-state concentration is 4 mg/L, and the drug's clearance is 2 L/h. Which infusion (dosing) rate will maintain this steady-state concentration?

explanation.summary: At steady state, dosing rate equals steady-state concentration multiplied by clearance.
