explanation.whyCorrect: Because Css = dosing rate / clearance, the dosing rate = Css x clearance = 4 mg/L x 2 L/h = 8 mg/h. At this rate the amount of drug entering the body equals the amount cleared, holding the concentration constant.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.5 mg/h is far too low and reflects dividing rather than multiplying the two values.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 2 mg/h equals the clearance alone and ignores the target concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 6 mg/h does not result from multiplying Css by clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 16 mg/h is double the correct rate and would drive the steady-state level above target.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A patient is given a single 200-mg dose of a drug that follows first-order elimination with a half-life of 5 hours. Assuming no further doses are given, approximately how much drug remains in the body after 10 hours?

explanation.summary: In first-order elimination, half the drug is removed during each half-life.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ten hours equals two half-lives. After one half-life (5 h) 100 mg remains, and after the second half-life (10 h) 50 mg remains.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 150 mg would represent removal of only one quarter of the dose, which does not match one half-life removal.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 100 mg is the amount remaining after a single half-life (5 hours), not two.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 25 mg is the amount remaining after three half-lives (15 hours).
whyWrongByChoice.E: The drug is not fully eliminated after two half-lives; a measurable amount remains.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A physician starts a patient on a fixed maintenance regimen of an orally administered drug that follows first-order elimination. Which of the following primarily determines the time required to reach steady-state concentration?

explanation.summary: Time to steady state is governed by the drug's elimination half-life, requiring roughly 4-5 half-lives.
explanation.whyCorrect: Steady state is attained after approximately 4-5 half-lives regardless of dose size or interval, so the elimination half-life sets how long it takes to reach steady state.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing the dose raises the steady-state concentration reached but does not change how long it takes to get there.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The dosing interval affects peak-to-trough swings, not the time to steady state.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The route of administration does not determine the time to steady state.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The peak-to-trough difference is a consequence of the dosing interval, not the determinant of time to steady state.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: An emergency physician observes that a patient's blood alcohol level falls at a constant 20 mg/dL per hour regardless of how high the initial level was. The same saturable pattern is seen with phenytoin and aspirin at high doses. Which term best describes this elimination pattern?

explanation.summary: Zero-order kinetics removes a constant amount of drug per unit time because the eliminating mechanism is saturated.
explanation.whyCorrect: A constant amount eliminated per unit time regardless of concentration defines zero-order kinetics. The mnemonic PEA (phenytoin, ethanol, aspirin) lists classic zero-order drugs whose elimination is saturable.

whyWrongByChoice.A: First-order kinetics removes a constant fraction, not a constant amount, so the rate would change with concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Redistribution describes a drug moving between tissue compartments, not a constant elimination rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: First-pass metabolism refers to hepatic extraction before a drug reaches systemic circulation, not the rate of ongoing elimination.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Enterohepatic recycling describes biliary secretion and reabsorption, not a fixed elimination rate.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A pharmacology student is comparing elimination kinetics. Which of the following statements best characterizes first-order elimination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A constant amount of drug is eliminated per unit time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A constant proportion (fraction) of drug is eliminated per unit time

choices.label: C
choices.text: The eliminating enzyme is saturated at all concentrations

choices.label: D
choices.text: A plot of drug concentration versus time is a straight line on linear axes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Half-life is not a meaningful parameter for the drug

explanation.summary: First-order elimination removes a constant fraction of the drug per unit time, so the absolute rate rises with concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In first-order kinetics the rate of elimination is proportional to the drug concentration, meaning a fixed percentage is cleared per unit time. This is the more common pattern and gives a straight line only when concentration is plotted on a logarithmic (ln) axis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A constant amount eliminated per unit time describes zero-order, not first-order, kinetics.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A saturated eliminating mechanism at all concentrations is a feature of zero-order kinetics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A straight line on linear axes describes zero-order elimination; first-order gives an exponential decay on linear axes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Half-life is a meaningful and defining parameter specifically for first-order drugs.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: Amiodarone, an antiarrhythmic drug, has a very large volume of distribution measured in hundreds of liters. Which of the following properties best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a large, charged molecule that is highly plasma-protein bound

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is confined to the intravascular compartment

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is lipophilic and accumulates extensively in body tissues, including fat

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is hydrophilic and remains within the extracellular fluid

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is rapidly and completely cleared by the kidneys

explanation.summary: A high volume of distribution reflects a lipophilic drug that is sequestered in tissues rather than staying in plasma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Drugs with a very high Vd are typically small lipophilic molecules that distribute into all tissues and are sequestered in compartments such as adipose tissue, leaving little drug measurable in the plasma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Large, charged, protein-bound molecules stay in the plasma and have a low Vd, not a high one.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A drug confined to the intravascular compartment has a low Vd near blood volume.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydrophilic drugs limited to extracellular fluid have an intermediate Vd, not hundreds of liters.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rapid renal clearance describes elimination and does not by itself produce a large volume of distribution.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A patient with severe heart failure is given a usual dose of a hydrophilic drug and develops a higher-than-expected peak plasma concentration. Which of the following best explains the elevated peak?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased volume of distribution from expanded tissue perfusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased renal clearance of the drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: A longer half-life caused by higher clearance

explanation.summary: In heart failure, low organ perfusion prevents wide distribution, reducing Vd and raising the peak concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: When organ perfusion is very low, the drug cannot distribute widely and more of it stays in the plasma. This reduces the volume of distribution and raises Cmax, so dose reduction is often needed in severe heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Perfusion is reduced, not expanded, in heart failure, so Vd falls rather than rises.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reduced blood flow in heart failure lowers rather than increases clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased first-pass metabolism would lower systemic exposure, not raise the peak concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Higher clearance shortens the half-life; here clearance is actually reduced.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: Compared with adults, neonates have a substantially higher proportion of body water. For a water-soluble (hydrophilic) drug, which of the following dose adjustments is most appropriate to achieve the target plasma concentration in a neonate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A lower dose because the volume of distribution is decreased

choices.label: B
choices.text: A higher dose because the volume of distribution is increased

choices.label: C
choices.text: No change because body water does not affect the volume of distribution

choices.label: D
choices.text: A longer dosing interval because clearance is markedly increased

choices.label: E
choices.text: A lower dose because plasma protein binding is increased

explanation.summary: Extra body water in neonates increases the Vd of hydrophilic drugs, diluting them and requiring a higher dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: For water-soluble drugs, the neonate's larger body water compartment increases the volume of distribution. The drug is diluted in the extra water, lowering the plasma concentration, so a higher dose is needed to reach the target level.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The volume of distribution for hydrophilic drugs is increased, not decreased, in neonates.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Body water directly affects the Vd of hydrophilic drugs, so a change is expected.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The dilution problem is one of volume of distribution, not increased clearance requiring a longer interval.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased protein binding is not the mechanism here; the dominant effect is dilution in expanded body water.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: Two patients each receive the same dose of a drug. Patient 1 is found to have a smaller area under the concentration-time curve (AUC) than Patient 2. Which of the following is most likely true of Patient 1?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patient 1 has a longer elimination half-life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patient 1 has a larger volume of distribution

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patient 1 has a higher steady-state concentration

explanation.summary: Because clearance equals dose divided by AUC, a smaller AUC at the same dose implies higher clearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Total clearance can be derived as CL = dose / AUC. For the same dose, a smaller AUC means more drug is being cleared, so Patient 1 has a higher clearance and reduced overall exposure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lower clearance would produce a larger AUC, the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Higher clearance tends to shorten, not lengthen, the elimination half-life.
whyWrongByChoice.D: AUC after a given dose reflects clearance; it is not determined by the volume of distribution.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Higher clearance lowers the steady-state concentration for a given dosing rate, so Css would be lower, not higher.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man taking several chronic medications develops daytime somnolence, orthostatic hypotension, and bradycardia. Laboratory monitoring shows that his drug levels are higher than expected for his prescribed doses. Which of the following age-related changes best explains the elevated drug levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased hepatic and renal blood flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduced organ blood flow and first-pass metabolism

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased total body water that dilutes all drugs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased volume of distribution for hydrophilic drugs

explanation.summary: Aging reduces organ blood flow and first-pass metabolism, slowing elimination and raising drug levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: With aging, diminished blood flow to eliminating organs and a less effective first-pass metabolism let more active drug reach and remain in the circulation, so elimination slows and levels rise. The Beers criteria list medications that are risky in older adults for this reason.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Organ blood flow decreases rather than increases with age, which slows elimination.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased protein synthesis is not the mechanism; the key change is reduced blood flow to eliminating organs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Total body water actually decreases with age, so dilution does not explain higher levels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An increased Vd for hydrophilic drugs would tend to lower peak levels, not raise steady-state drug concentrations.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A physician caring for a patient on a continuous intravenous infusion asks whether doubling the infusion rate will allow the drug to reach steady state in half the usual time. Which of the following is the most accurate response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes; doubling the infusion rate halves the time to steady state

choices.label: B
choices.text: No; time to steady state depends on the elimination half-life

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; steady state is reached after exactly two half-lives regardless of infusion rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: No; steady state can never be reached during a continuous infusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: No; doubling the infusion rate doubles the time to steady state

explanation.summary: Time to steady state is fixed by the elimination half-life; changing the infusion rate changes only the level reached.
explanation.whyCorrect: Steady state is attained after roughly 4-5 half-lives independent of the infusion rate. Doubling the rate raises the eventual steady-state concentration but does not shorten the time needed to get there.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The infusion rate does not affect the time to steady state, so it cannot halve it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Steady state requires about 4-5 half-lives, not two, and the number is set by half-life rather than infusion rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Steady state is reached during a continuous infusion once input and elimination balance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Doubling the infusion rate does not lengthen the time to steady state either; the time is governed by half-life.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-2: Volume of Distribution and Elimination Kinetics

questions.stem: A physician wants to rapidly achieve a therapeutic plasma concentration of a drug that has a large apparent volume of distribution. When calculating the appropriate loading dose, which pharmacokinetic parameter is the primary determinant?

explanation.summary: A loading dose is calculated from the target concentration and the volume of distribution (LD = Cp x Vd / F).
explanation.whyCorrect: The loading dose fills the drug's apparent volume of distribution to immediately reach the target steady-state concentration, so Vd is the key determinant. The larger the Vd, the larger the loading dose needed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Clearance is the primary determinant of the maintenance dose, not the loading dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The dosage interval (tau) appears in the maintenance dose equation, not the loading dose.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Half-life governs how long it takes to reach steady state, not the size of the loading dose.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal excretion rate is not a variable in the loading dose formula.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A patient on a continuous IV infusion needs a maintenance dose calculated to keep the plasma concentration at steady state. Which parameter most directly determines the required maintenance dose?

explanation.summary: The maintenance dose is driven by clearance (MD = Cp x CL x tau / F).
explanation.whyCorrect: A maintenance dose must replace the amount of drug the body clears over each interval, so clearance is the primary determinant. This is why the brick lists 'dependent on clearance' as the defining feature of a maintenance dose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Volume of distribution determines the loading dose, not the maintenance dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The size of the prior loading dose does not set the maintenance dose; the maintenance dose replaces what is cleared.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Half-life relates to the time to reach steady state rather than the maintenance dose amount.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Body surface area is not a term in the maintenance dose equation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A drug is given as an IV bolus, so its bioavailability (F) is 1.0. The target steady-state plasma concentration is 50 mg/mL and the volume of distribution is 5 mL. What is the appropriate loading dose?

explanation.summary: Loading dose = Cp x Vd / F.
explanation.whyCorrect: LD = (50 mg/mL x 5 mL) / 1.0 = 250 mg, matching the fauxspirin IV example in the brick.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 25 mg would result from dividing Cp by Vd rather than multiplying.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 125 mg is half of the correct value and does not follow the formula.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 500 mg would result if F were mistakenly set to 0.5 instead of 1.0 for an IV drug.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 1000 mg is off by a factor of four and does not follow the formula.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A patient is started on oral digoxin for atrial fibrillation. The bioavailability (F) is 0.6, the volume of distribution is 500 L, and the target peak plasma concentration is 1.5 mcg/L. Which oral loading dose will rapidly achieve this concentration?

explanation.summary: Loading dose = Cp x Vd / F, with careful attention to units.
explanation.whyCorrect: LD = (1.5 mcg/L x 500 L) / 0.6 = 1250 mcg = 1.25 mg. This is the digoxin loading dose from the brick's review question.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 1250 is the correct number but expressed in the wrong unit (mg instead of mcg).
whyWrongByChoice.C: 1.50 mg does not follow from the calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 1.0 g (1000 mg) is far larger than the calculated dose and uses the wrong unit.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 2.25 mg results from an incorrect calculation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A drug given by continuous IV infusion has a steady-state plasma concentration of 40 mg/mL, and the drug is being cleared at 20 mL/h. What rate of drug administration (in mg/h) maintains this steady state?

explanation.summary: At steady state, rate of drug intake = steady-state concentration x clearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rate = 40 mg/mL x 20 mL/h = 800 mg/h. If you know the steady-state concentration you can multiply by clearance to get the required infusion rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 2 mg/h divides the steady-state concentration by clearance instead of multiplying.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 4 mg/h is an incorrect calculation far below the needed rate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50 mg/h is too small to maintain the target steady-state concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 200 mg/h is only a quarter of the required infusion rate.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A seizing patient in the emergency department requires immediate therapeutic drug levels, so the physician administers a loading dose. What is the primary benefit of giving a loading dose?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elimination of the need for a maintenance dose

explanation.summary: A loading dose quickly reaches the target steady-state concentration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Administering a loading dose allows the drug to reach the target steady-state concentration with essentially one administration, rather than waiting several half-lives.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A loading dose is a single large initial dose, not a gradual escalation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reducing side effects is not a primary benefit; larger doses can actually raise toxicity risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A loading dose does not change how often the drug must be given afterward.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A maintenance dose is still required after the loading dose to sustain steady state.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with atrial fibrillation is prescribed oral amiodarone 800 mg daily for six weeks, after which the dose is changed to 200 mg daily. Amiodarone is highly lipophilic with a very large volume of distribution and incomplete oral absorption. Which of the following best explains why the daily dose was lowered after six weeks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The initial high dose served as a loading dose to fill the large volume of distribution

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pharmacologic tolerance develops, so a lower dose gives the same effect

choices.label: C
choices.text: The drug's clearance decreases dramatically after six weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal excretion of amiodarone increases over time

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral bioavailability rises to 100% after six weeks

explanation.summary: The high initial amiodarone dose is a loading dose; it is then reduced to a maintenance dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because amiodarone is lipophilic with a large Vd (~66 L/kg) and slow, variable absorption, a high loading dose (800 mg) is used to build up tissue levels, followed by a lower maintenance dose (200 mg) to hold the steady-state concentration and prevent toxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The change reflects a switch from loading to maintenance dosing, not pharmacologic tolerance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The brick does not attribute the change to a drop in clearance; it reflects the loading-to-maintenance transition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased renal excretion is not the reason the dose was lowered.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral amiodarone absorption is incomplete and variable (about 30-70%), not 100%.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: An emergency physician gives a seizing patient an IV phenytoin loading dose but does not reduce the dose afterward. The patient develops somnolence, severe ataxia, and dysarthria, with a serum phenytoin level three times the maximum therapeutic level. Which error most likely caused this toxicity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure to switch from the loading dose to a maintenance dose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infusing the loading dose too slowly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using the intravenous rather than the oral route

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administering a loading dose that was too small

choices.label: E
choices.text: Selecting a drug with too short a half-life

explanation.summary: Continuing a loading-level dose instead of switching to a maintenance dose causes accumulation and toxicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes exactly this preventable error: the loading dose was not switched to an appropriate maintenance dose, so serum phenytoin rose to three times the maximum therapeutic level, producing somnolence, ataxia, and dysarthria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The problem was continued high dosing, not the infusion rate of the initial dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The IV route is appropriate for rapidly achieving therapeutic levels in a seizing patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The dose that accumulated was excessive, not too small.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A short half-life was not described and would not explain the accumulation.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A drug is started at its regular maintenance dose without a loading dose. Its elimination half-life is about 1 day. Approximately how long will it take to reach a steady-state concentration?

explanation.summary: Roughly 4 to 5 half-lives are needed to reach steady state with regular dosing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that about 4 to 5 half-lives are needed to reach the steady-state concentration; with a half-life of 1 day, that is roughly 4 to 5 days.

whyWrongByChoice.A: One half-life reaches only about 50% of steady state.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Two half-lives reach only about 75% of steady state.
whyWrongByChoice.D: About 10 days is far more than the 4 to 5 half-lives required.
whyWrongByChoice.E: About 30 days greatly overestimates the time to steady state for a 1-day half-life.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: When calculating a loading or maintenance dose, which value is used for bioavailability (F) when a drug is administered intravenously?

explanation.summary: For IV drugs, bioavailability is 1.0 because the drug reaches the circulation immediately.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that F equals 1.0 when a drug is given intravenously, because the entire dose reaches the systemic circulation without first-pass loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0.3 is near the low end of amiodarone's incomplete oral absorption, not an IV value.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 0.5 is an example of reduced oral bioavailability, not the IV value.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 0.7 is the estimate used for oral drugs, reflecting hepatic first-pass metabolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bioavailability cannot exceed 1.0.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A patient is receiving a drug by continuous IV infusion and has reached steady state. If the infusion rate is doubled while the infusion time is unchanged, what happens to the steady-state plasma concentration?

explanation.summary: Steady-state concentration is directly proportional to the infusion rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that doubling the infusion rate of a drug without changing the infusion time doubles the steady-state concentration, because Css is proportional to the rate of drug input.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing the input rate must change the steady-state concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Halving would occur if the rate were reduced, not doubled.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The relationship is linear, so doubling the rate doubles (not quadruples) the concentration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increasing the infusion rate cannot drive the concentration to zero.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: Two formulations of the same drug target the same steady-state concentration and have the same volume of distribution. Formulation X is given IV (F = 1.0) and formulation Y is given orally (F = 0.5). How does the required oral loading dose of Y compare with the IV loading dose of X?

explanation.summary: Because the loading dose is divided by F, lower bioavailability requires a proportionally larger dose.
explanation.whyCorrect: Loading dose = Cp x Vd / F. With Cp and Vd equal, the only difference is F: the oral dose is divided by 0.5 instead of 1.0, so it must be 1/0.5 = 2 times the IV dose. This mirrors the fauxspirin examples (250 mg IV vs 1000 mg orally when F fell to 0.5 and Cp doubled).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lower bioavailability requires more drug, not less.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The doses cannot be equal because a portion of the oral dose does not reach the circulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: F = 0.5 requires only twice the dose; four times would correspond to F = 0.25.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A one-quarter dose would apply if bioavailability were higher, not lower.

questions.topicName: Pharmacokinetics-3: Loading and Maintenance Dose

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is given a standard weight-based dose of a medication that is cleared by hepatic CYP metabolism. He develops signs of drug toxicity, and serum levels are far higher than expected. Genotyping shows he has essentially no functional activity of the metabolizing enzyme. Which of the following best describes his metabolizer phenotype?

choices.label: E
choices.text: This reflects ethnicity, not a metabolizer phenotype

explanation.summary: Poor metabolizers have little to no enzyme activity, so active drug accumulates and toxicity risk is highest.
explanation.whyCorrect: Poor metabolizers (PMs) have little or no functional enzyme activity and clear a drug substrate very slowly. This leads to higher serum drug levels and the greatest risk of dose-related toxicity, matching the vignette of markedly elevated levels and no functional enzyme.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ultra-rapid metabolizers have increased enzyme activity and metabolize drug too quickly, giving subtherapeutic (not toxic) levels of an active drug.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Extensive metabolizers have normal enzyme activity and the expected drug response, not accumulation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intermediate metabolizers have reduced but still-present activity; the vignette specifies essentially no functional enzyme, which defines poor metabolizers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ethnicity influences how common a variant is, but the phenotype here is defined by absent enzyme activity, i.e., poor metabolizer.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A patient started on warfarin requires an unusually low dose to reach a therapeutic INR and is very sensitive to the drug. A polymorphism in the enzyme responsible for metabolizing warfarin is suspected. Which cytochrome P450 enzyme is most commonly associated with warfarin metabolism?

explanation.summary: Warfarin is a classic CYP2C9 substrate, and CYP2C9 polymorphisms alter warfarin dosing.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick identifies warfarin as a substrate metabolized by CYP2C9 (with VKORC1 also relevant). Variants in CYP2C9 slow warfarin metabolism, raising drug levels and bleeding risk, so genotype affects the appropriate dose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP3A4 metabolizes the largest share of common drugs but is not the enzyme classically paired with warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CYP2D6 is associated with drugs such as codeine, not warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CYP1A2 is associated with substrates such as caffeine, not warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2C19 is the enzyme classically linked to clopidogrel, not warfarin.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man newly diagnosed with HIV is going to begin an antiretroviral regimen that includes abacavir. To reduce the risk of a severe, potentially life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction, which of the following tests is required before starting therapy?

explanation.summary: HLA-B*5701 testing is required before abacavir to prevent hypersensitivity reactions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists abacavir among the medications for which pharmacogenomic testing is required before initiation. HLA-B*5701-positive patients should not receive abacavir because of a markedly increased risk of a severe hypersensitivity reaction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP2C19 testing relates to clopidogrel, not abacavir hypersensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS mutation testing guides EGFR-inhibitor therapy such as cetuximab.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HER2 gene expression is tested to guide trastuzumab, not abacavir.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2D6 phenotype relates to drugs such as codeine, not abacavir hypersensitivity.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A patient stable on a medication metabolized by CYP3A4 is started on rifampin for tuberculosis. Over the following weeks the original medication becomes progressively less effective, and its serum concentration falls. Which of the following best explains this interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rifampin inhibits CYP3A4, slowing metabolism of the drug

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rifampin displaces the drug from plasma proteins, increasing clearance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rifampin blocks renal tubular secretion of the drug

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rifampin converts the drug into an inactive prodrug in the gut

explanation.summary: An inducer like rifampin increases hepatic CYP activity, so substrate drugs are cleared faster and become less effective.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick teaches that an inducer increases the rate of hepatic metabolism, lowering serum concentrations of drugs metabolized by that CYP enzyme; those drugs are cleared more quickly and may become less effective. Rifampin is a classic CYP3A4 inducer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inhibition would slow metabolism and raise drug levels, the opposite of the observed loss of effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The mechanism described in the brick is enzyme induction, not protein-binding displacement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The interaction is hepatic CYP induction, not a renal excretion effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rifampin induces CYP enzymes; it does not convert an active drug into an inactive prodrug.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman on chronic warfarin therapy is prescribed trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a urinary tract infection. Several days later her INR is markedly elevated and she develops bleeding. Which of the following best explains this interaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole induces CYP2C9, increasing warfarin metabolism

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole inhibits CYP2C9, decreasing warfarin metabolism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warfarin induces the metabolism of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is an inducer that lowers warfarin serum concentration

choices.label: E
choices.text: The antibiotic accelerates renal clearance of warfarin

explanation.summary: An inhibitor of CYP2C9 slows warfarin metabolism, raising its concentration and bleeding risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin is a CYP2C9 substrate, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is a CYP2C9 inhibitor. By slowing warfarin metabolism, the inhibitor raises warfarin concentrations, increasing INR and bleeding risk, which the brick highlights as a classic clinically significant interaction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Induction would lower warfarin levels and reduce, not increase, INR and bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The clinically important effect is the antibiotic inhibiting warfarin metabolism, not warfarin acting on the antibiotic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The antibiotic is an inhibitor here; an inducer would decrease warfarin levels and would not cause bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mechanism taught is hepatic CYP2C9 inhibition, not accelerated renal clearance.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: Pharmacogenomic variability in drug response is largely attributable to single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs). Which of the following best describes a SNP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A change in a single nucleotide in the genetic sequence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deletion of an entire gene from a chromosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: A reversible methylation change with no sequence alteration

explanation.summary: A SNP is a change in a single nucleotide in the genetic sequence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick defines a single nucleotide polymorphism as a change in a single nucleotide (for example, C replaced by G). SNPs are responsible for the majority of individual variability in drug response, including polymorphic CYP450 enzymes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A whole-gene deletion is a much larger structural change than a single-nucleotide substitution.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromosomal duplication involves entire chromosomes, not a single nucleotide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Inserting multiple genes is a large-scale change, not a single-nucleotide polymorphism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methylation is an epigenetic modification and does not alter the nucleotide sequence, which a SNP does.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A patient is genotyped as an ultra-rapid metabolizer for the CYP enzyme that clears an active (non-prodrug) analgesic she takes. Despite standard dosing, she reports inadequate pain relief. Which of the following best explains her poor response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced enzyme activity causes the drug to accumulate to toxic levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: The drug is metabolized too quickly, producing subtherapeutic levels

choices.label: C
choices.text: The drug cannot be absorbed from the gut

choices.label: D
choices.text: The enzyme converts the drug into a more potent metabolite

choices.label: E
choices.text: She has no functional copy of the metabolizing enzyme

explanation.summary: Ultra-rapid metabolizers clear an active drug too fast, giving low levels and reduced effect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that ultra-rapid metabolizers metabolize a drug too quickly, so an active drug may not reach effective concentrations and has less therapeutic effect. This explains inadequate analgesia at a standard dose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Accumulation and toxicity describe poor or intermediate metabolizers, not ultra-rapid metabolizers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The issue is rapid metabolism, not impaired absorption.
whyWrongByChoice.D: For an active (non-prodrug) analgesic, rapid metabolism reduces the active drug rather than creating a more potent metabolite.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Having no functional enzyme describes a poor metabolizer, the opposite of ultra-rapid.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with metastatic colorectal cancer is being considered for cetuximab, an EGFR-inhibitor monoclonal antibody. Which of the following findings would predict that this therapy is unlikely to be effective and should not be used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: EGFR present on the tumor cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: A KRAS mutation in the tumor

explanation.summary: A KRAS mutation predicts that EGFR-inhibitor therapy such as cetuximab will not be effective.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that cetuximab binds EGFR on tumor cells, but if the patient has a KRAS mutation (KRAS-positive), therapy with cetuximab will not be effective and should not be used. KRAS testing therefore guides use of this drug.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Presence of EGFR is the target of the drug and does not by itself predict failure; KRAS mutation status does.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HER2 amplification guides trastuzumab therapy, not cetuximab effectiveness.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HLA-B*5701 relates to abacavir hypersensitivity, not cetuximab response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2C9 status affects drugs like warfarin, not the tumor response to an EGFR inhibitor.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: Certain HLA-B allele variants are associated with severe, sometimes fatal, drug hypersensitivity reactions. The HLA-B gene is a component of which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The VKORC1 vitamin K cycle complex

explanation.summary: HLA-B is part of the major histocompatibility complex and helps code the immune response.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that human leukocyte antigen B (HLA-B) is a member of the major histocompatibility complex (MHC), important for coding the immune response and determining which peptides are presented. Variant HLA-B alleles can drive severe hypersensitivity reactions to certain drugs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cytochrome P450 enzymes handle drug metabolism and are distinct from the immune HLA-B gene.
whyWrongByChoice.C: VKORC1 relates to warfarin sensitivity, not to antigen presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: EGFR signaling is a tumor pathway targeted by drugs like cetuximab, unrelated to HLA-B.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ABC transporters move substrates across membranes and are not part of the MHC.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man who received a coronary stent and is taking clopidogrel has a recurrent thrombotic event despite good adherence. A polymorphism reducing conversion of clopidogrel to its active form is suspected. A poor-metabolizer variant of which enzyme is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: Clopidogrel is associated with CYP2C19, and poor-metabolizer variants reduce its effectiveness.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick pairs clopidogrel with CYP2C19. Reduced CYP2C19 activity lowers activation of clopidogrel, blunting its antiplatelet effect and increasing the risk of thrombotic events, which fits this stent patient's recurrent thrombosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CYP2C9 is associated with warfarin, not clopidogrel activation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CYP2D6 is associated with drugs such as codeine, not clopidogrel.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CYP3A4 metabolizes many drugs but is not the enzyme the brick pairs with clopidogrel.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP1A2 is associated with substrates such as caffeine, not clopidogrel.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A patient taking a medication metabolized by CYP3A4 develops elevated drug levels and side effects after regularly drinking grapefruit juice. With respect to CYP3A4, grapefruit juice is acting as which of the following?

choices.label: E
choices.text: An enhancer of renal drug excretion

explanation.summary: Grapefruit juice inhibits CYP3A4, slowing metabolism and raising levels of CYP3A4 substrates.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists grapefruit juice among CYP3A4 inhibitors. By inhibiting CYP3A4, it slows metabolism of substrate drugs so their serum concentrations rise, increasing the potential for toxicity, consistent with the elevated levels and side effects described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An inducer would lower drug levels; the patient's levels rose, indicating inhibition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Grapefruit juice is listed as a CYP3A4 inhibitor, not a CYP2C9 inducer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The drug is a CYP3A4 substrate, and grapefruit juice acts on CYP3A4, not CYP1A2.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mechanism is hepatic CYP3A4 inhibition, not enhanced renal excretion.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics

questions.stem: A 49-year-old woman with newly diagnosed breast cancer is being evaluated for trastuzumab therapy. Which of the following tests is required before starting this medication?

explanation.summary: Trastuzumab requires HER2 gene expression testing before therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists trastuzumab among the drugs for which testing is required before starting therapy, using HER2 gene expression. Because trastuzumab targets the HER2 pathway, therapy is directed by HER2 status.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HLA-B*5701 testing is required for abacavir, not trastuzumab.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CYP2C19 genotype relates to clopidogrel, not trastuzumab.
whyWrongByChoice.D: KRAS mutation testing guides EGFR-inhibitor therapy such as cetuximab.
whyWrongByChoice.E: CYP2D6 phenotype relates to drugs such as codeine, not trastuzumab.

questions.topicName: Pharmacodynamics 3 - Pharmacogenomics
